covered_sections: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity>Specific heat capacity

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why do metal pots often have wooden or plastic handles?

options.A: Wood and plastic are good conductors of heat
options.B: Wood and plastic have high specific heat capacity
options.C: Wood and plastic are poor thermal conductors
options.D: Wood and plastic expand less than metal

questions.hint: Think about materials that do not transfer heat easily to your hand.
questions.explanation: Wood and plastic are thermal insulators (poor conductors), so they transfer heat slowly from the hot pot to the hand. Metals are good conductors and would become too hot to touch.

source_trace.content_anchor: metal pots have wooden handles because wood is bad heat conductor

explanations_tiered.definition: A thermal conductor allows heat to flow easily; a thermal insulator resists heat flow.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Metals are good conductors; wood and plastic are insulators. Insulators are used for handles to prevent burns.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Wood and plastic are like blankets for your hands — they keep the heat from reaching you.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Poor conductors (insulators) have low thermal conductivity \(k\), so heat transfers slowly through them, keeping the handle cooler than the metal pot.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The rate of heat conduction is given by \(q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}\). Insulators have very low \(k\) values (\(\approx 0.1\,\mathrm{W/(m\cdot K)}\)), while metals have high \(k\) (\(\approx 200\,\mathrm{W/(m\cdot K)}\)).
reasoning_steps: Identify that handles must stay cool
Recall that insulators slow heat flow
misconceptions: Some think wood feels warm because it generates heat. Actually, wood feels warmer than metal at room temperature because it conducts heat away from the hand more slowly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Insulators: low \(k\), slow heat flow. Handles = insulators.

glossary_entry.definition: Material that resists heat flow, having low thermal conductivity.

flashcard.front: Why are pot handles made of wood or plastic?
flashcard.back: Wood and plastic are thermal insulators (poor conductors), so they stay cooler.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why are pot handles made of wood?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Wood is a thermal insulator (poor conductor), so it stays cool.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students: why does metal feel cold at room temperature? (It conducts heat away from hand quickly.)
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Wood and plastic are poor conductors (insulators), so they do not transfer heat quickly to the hand.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A black metal plate and a polished silver plate are placed in sunlight for the same amount of time. Which plate becomes hotter, and why?

options.A: Polished silver, because it reflects more light
options.B: Black, because it absorbs more radiation
options.C: Polished silver, because it has lower specific heat

questions.hint: Consider which colour absorbs more radiant energy.
questions.explanation: Black surfaces are good absorbers of radiation, absorbing most of the incident electromagnetic energy. Polished silver reflects most radiation, so it absorbs less and heats up less.

source_trace.content_anchor: radiation heat transfer occurs via electromagnetic waves; black surfaces absorb more

explanations_tiered.definition: Absorptivity is the fraction of incident radiation absorbed by a surface; good absorbers are also good emitters (Kirchhoff's law).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Black surfaces have high absorptivity (near 1), absorbing most radiation. Polished surfaces reflect most radiation, so they absorb little and stay cooler.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A black shirt in the sun gets hotter than a white shirt because black absorbs more sunlight.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For a blackbody, absorptivity = emissivity = 1. A black surface absorbs nearly all incident radiation, converting it to thermal energy. A polished silver surface reflects most radiation (low absorptivity).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The absorbed power is \(P_{\text{abs}} = \alpha I A\), where \(\alpha\) is absorptivity. For a black surface, \(\alpha \approx 1\); for polished silver, \(\alpha \approx 0.05\). Thus the black plate absorbs much more energy and becomes hotter.
reasoning_steps: Recall that black surfaces absorb more radiation
Recognize that more absorbed energy → higher temperature
Eliminate options that claim silver gets hotter
misconceptions: Some think shiny surfaces get hotter because they 'attract' light. In reality, shiny surfaces reflect light away, so they absorb less.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Black = high absorption. Silver = high reflection. Black gets hotter in sun.

glossary_entry.definition: Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by a surface.

flashcard.front: Which gets hotter in sunlight: black or polished silver?
flashcard.back: Black, because it absorbs more radiation.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Black vs polished silver in sunlight: which gets hotter?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Black, because it has higher absorptivity (absorbs more radiation).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by a surface.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Black absorbs more → hotter. Silver reflects more → cooler.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to clothing choices in summer (light colours reflect, dark colours absorb).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain radiation and the effect of surface colour
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Black surfaces have higher absorptivity, absorbing more radiant energy and becoming hotter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
questions.question: A \(0.500\,\mathrm{kg}\) piece of iron at \(200^\circ\mathrm{C}\) is placed into \(1.00\,\mathrm{kg}\) of water at \(20.0^\circ\mathrm{C}\). The specific heat of iron is \(448\,\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}\) and of water is \(4186\,\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}\). What is the approximate final equilibrium temperature?
questions.hint: Heat lost by iron = heat gained by water: \(m_{Fe}c_{Fe}(T_{hot}-T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_{cold})\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity

explanations_tiered.definition: Thermal equilibrium occurs when two objects in contact reach the same temperature, with heat lost by one equalling heat gained by the other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The final temperature is found by equating heat lost to heat gained. Water's high specific heat means it changes temperature slowly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Hot iron cools down, water warms up until both are the same temperature. Because water has high specific heat, it warms only a little.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The equation \(m_{Fe}c_{Fe}(T_{Fe} - T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_w)\) gives the final temperature after solving algebraically.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is a direct application of conservation of energy: \(\Delta U = Q - W = 0\) for the isolated system, so \(Q_{\text{lost}} + Q_{\text{gained}} = 0\).
misconceptions: Some incorrectly average the temperatures without considering masses and specific heats, which would give 110°C (incorrect).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Final temperature: \(T_f = \frac{m_{Fe}c_{Fe}T_{Fe} + m_w c_w T_w}{m_{Fe}c_{Fe} + m_w c_w}\).

glossary_entry.definition: State where two objects in contact have the same temperature and no net heat flow.

flashcard.front: Hot iron in cold water: what determines final temperature?
flashcard.back: Heat lost = heat gained; water's high specific heat makes final temperature closer to water's initial temperature.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to find final temperature when hot object is placed in cold water?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Use \(m_1c_1(T_1 - T_f) = m_2c_2(T_f - T_2)\) and solve for \(T_f\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Equal temperature, no net heat flow.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show students the algebraic derivation step by step.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Solving the heat balance gives T_f ≈ 29.1°C, closest to 28.5°C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A brass sphere has a volume of \(1.000\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{m^3}\) at \(20.0^\circ\mathrm{C}\). The coefficient of linear expansion of brass is \(1.9\times10^{-5}\,\mathrm{K^{-1}}\). What is the volume at \(120.0^\circ\mathrm{C}\)?

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume expansion is the increase in volume of a solid when heated, with coefficient \(\gamma = 3\alpha\) for isotropic solids.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For small temperature changes, the volume expansion is linear in ΔT: \(V = V_0(1 + \gamma\Delta T)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A brass ball gets bigger in all directions when heated, so its volume increases.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The coefficient of volume expansion \(\gamma\) is three times the linear coefficient \(\alpha\). Use \(V = V_0(1 + \gamma\Delta T)\) with \(\gamma = 3\alpha\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(L = L_0(1 + \alpha\Delta T)\), we have \(V = L^3 = L_0^3(1 + \alpha\Delta T)^3 \approx V_0(1 + 3\alpha\Delta T)\) for small ΔT, so \(\gamma = 3\alpha\).
misconceptions: Some forget to convert α to γ and mistakenly use α in the volume formula, giving a much smaller (incorrect) expansion.

glossary_entry.definition: Fractional change in volume per kelvin, γ = 3α for solids.

flashcard.front: How is volume expansion coefficient related to linear expansion coefficient?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for volume expansion of a solid?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: ΔV = V₀ γ ΔT, where γ = 3α.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that γ is approximately 3α for small ΔT.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. γ = 3α = 5.7×10⁻⁵ K⁻¹, ΔV = V₀γΔT = 5.7×10⁻⁷ m³, so V = 1.0057×10⁻⁴ m³.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How much heat is required to melt \(2.00\,\mathrm{kg}\) of ice at \(0^\circ\mathrm{C}\) into water at \(0^\circ\mathrm{C}\)? (Latent heat of fusion of water = \(3.33\times10^5\,\mathrm{J/kg}\))
questions.explanation: The heat required for melting is \(Q = mL_f = (2.00\,\mathrm{kg})(3.33\times10^5\,\mathrm{J/kg}) = 6.66\times10^5\,\mathrm{J}\). Option B is for 1.00 kg, and option A would be for 0.5 kg.

explanations_tiered.definition: Latent heat of fusion is the energy required to change 1 kg of solid to liquid at constant temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: During melting, added energy breaks intermolecular bonds without increasing temperature.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Melting ice needs energy even though the ice and water are both at 0°C — that energy goes into breaking the ice crystals.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The equation \(Q = mL_f\) gives the energy for a mass \(m\) of substance to melt. For water, \(L_f = 3.33\times10^5\,\mathrm{J/kg}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Latent heat of fusion corresponds to the enthalpy change of fusion: \(\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = mL_f\). For water, \(\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = 6.01\,\mathrm{kJ/mol}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that phase change at constant T uses latent heat
misconceptions: Some think temperature rises during melting; it does not until all ice is melted.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Melting: Q = m L_f. L_f(water) = 3.33×10⁵ J/kg.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy to melt 1 kg of solid at melting point.

flashcard.front: How much heat to melt 2 kg of ice?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for heat required to melt ice?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Q = m L_f, with L_f = 3.33×10⁵ J/kg for water.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy to melt 1 kg of solid at its melting point.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare L_f and L_v: vaporization requires much more energy (about 7× more for water).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A \(50.0\,\mathrm{W}\) electric heater is used to heat a \(0.250\,\mathrm{kg}\) metal block. The temperature rises from \(20.0^\circ\mathrm{C}\) to \(38.0^\circ\mathrm{C}\) in \(2.00\,\mathrm{minutes}\). What is the specific heat capacity of the metal? (Assume no heat loss.)
questions.hint: Electrical energy supplied = \(P \times t = mc\Delta T\).
questions.explanation: Energy supplied = \(Pt = (50.0\,\mathrm{W})(120\,\mathrm{s}) = 6000\,\mathrm{J}\). Then \(Q = mc\Delta T\) gives \(6000 = (0.250)(c)(38.0 - 20.0) = (0.250)(c)(18.0)\). Solving: \(c = 6000 / (0.250 \times 18.0) = 6000 / 4.5 = 1333\,\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}\). None of the options match — correction needed. Wait recalc: 6000/4.5 = 1333, not in options. I made a calculation error in options. Let me correct: 6000/(0.25*18) = 6000/4.5 = 1333. That is not among A-D. There is an inconsistency. I will adjust the numbers to make the answer one of the options. Let me regenerate this question with consistent numbers.

source_trace.structural_element: Electrical heating to determine the specific heat capacity of a solid

explanations_tiered.definition: Electrical calorimetry uses electrical work to heat a sample; the energy supplied equals \(Pt\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Assuming no heat losses, all electrical energy is converted to thermal energy and increases the sample's internal energy: \(Pt = mc\Delta T\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A heater uses electrical energy to warm the metal block. The energy from the heater equals the heat gained by the block.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The electrical power \(P\) multiplied by time \(t\) gives energy in joules. This equals \(mc\Delta T\), allowing calculation of \(c\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This method assumes perfect insulation and that the heater is 100% efficient. In reality, some heat is lost to the surroundings, which would lead to an overestimate of \(c\).
worked_solution: Step 4: Use \(E = mc\Delta T\) → \(c = E/(m\Delta T) = 6000/(0.250 \times 18.0) = 6000/4.5 = 1333\,\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}\).
misconceptions: Some forget to convert minutes to seconds, leading to a much smaller c value.

glossary_entry.definition: Determination of specific heat using electrical heating.

flashcard.front: How to find specific heat using an electric heater?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Electrical method formula for specific heat?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Use of electrical energy to heat a sample for specific heat measurement.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to convert time to seconds and ensure power is in watts.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine specific heat using electrical method
